title: Pizza napoletana
subtitle: Slow-risen Neapolitan dough baked at the highest heat your oven can produce
excerpt: The Neapolitan pizza: a thin, soft, leopard-spotted base with a puffy, charred cornicione, San Marzano tomato sauce, and fresh mozzarella. The dough uses a cold, slow fermentation over 24 to 48 hours, which develops flavour and produces a light, open crumb.
sourceNotes: Neapolitan pizza is documented from Naples from the late eighteenth century. The Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (AVPN) codified the rules in 1984; the specification became a European TSG (Traditional Speciality Guaranteed) in 2010. Method follows the standard AVPN cold-fermentation approach adapted for a domestic oven.

content.text: Neapolitan pizza is not defined by toppings. It is defined by the dough. Cold-fermented over 24 to 48 hours, made from high-protein flour, stretched by hand until it is almost see-through at the centre, and baked in under 90 seconds in a wood-fired oven at 480°C. A home oven at full heat needs 8 to 10 minutes. A pizza steel that gets ripping hot before the pizza goes on helps a lot.

The tomato sauce is uncooked: crushed San Marzano tomatoes, a pinch of salt, and nothing else. Cooking the sauce before it goes on the pizza risks reducing it too much in the oven. The mozzarella, fior di latte or (if you can find it) mozzarella di bufala, is torn rather than sliced, dried on kitchen paper for 10 minutes before using.

items.name: Mozzarella
items.prepNote: drained and torn, dried on kitchen paper

content.text: Mix and rest the dough (day before)

Combine the {{00-flour}} of flour and {{water}} of cold water in a large bowl and mix until no dry flour remains. Cover and rest for 30 minutes. Add the {{yeast-fast-action}} of yeast and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Divide into 4 equal balls (about 210 g each). Place each ball in a lightly oiled container with a lid and refrigerate for 24 to 48 hours.
Bring to room temperature and heat the oven

Remove the dough balls from the fridge 2 hours before baking. Place a pizza steel or stone on the top shelf of the oven and heat to the maximum temperature, 270 to 300°C if your oven allows. Allow at least 45 minutes for the steel or stone to get fully hot.

Crush the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes by hand and season with a pinch of salt. Flour a work surface generously. Flatten a dough ball with your palm, then use your fingertips to press outward from the centre, leaving a 2 cm border for the cornicione. Pick the dough up and stretch it over your fists, rotating gently, until it is about 28 cm across and almost translucent at the centre. Lay on a floured pizza peel or the back of a baking tray.

Spread 80 ml of the tomato sauce over the base with the back of a spoon, leaving the rim bare. Scatter over a quarter of the {{mozzarella}} mozzarella. Drizzle with a little of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Slide the pizza onto the hot steel or stone and bake for 8 to 10 minutes until the base is blistered and the edges are charred in places. Tear {{basil}} of basil leaves over the top and serve immediately.

items.fix: Allow the dough to rest at room temperature for the full 2 hours; if it tears, cover it and rest it for 15 more minutes before trying again
items.cause: Not rested long enough after coming out of the fridge, or gluten not fully developed

items.fix: Use the maximum oven temperature; preheat the steel for at least 45 to 60 minutes; bake on the highest shelf
items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or steel not preheated for long enough

items.fix: Drain and tear the mozzarella, then lay it on kitchen paper for 10 minutes before topping the pizza
items.symptom: Mozzarella has released a pool of water

items.fix: Flour the peel generously; once the pizza is on the peel, work quickly. It should go in the oven within 2 minutes
items.cause: Not enough flour on the peel, or too long sitting before going in the oven
items.symptom: Pizza has stuck to the peel

content.text: Pizza from Naples is one of the most specific dishes in the world. There is a formal spec covering the flour type, the rise time, the oven temperature, and the diameter of the disc. The Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana, founded in 1984, guards the definition. Whether that matters for a home cook is another question. But the cold fermentation and the screaming-hot oven do produce a meaningfully different pizza from the same-day, low-temperature approach. The difference shows up in the crust: open, slightly chewy, with a proper char.
